title: Bottom-Up vs Top-Down Clustering
concept: Agglomerative clustering starts with every point as its own cluster and merges upward, while divisive clustering starts with one giant cluster and splits downward — agglomerative is far more common in practice.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do you call the tree diagram produced by hierarchical clustering?
exercises.explain: Right! A dendrogram records every merge, and today you'll learn the two directions you can build it from.

cards.title: Two ways to build the same tower
cards.body: You can build a Lego tower two ways: stack individual bricks one at a time until you reach the top, or start with a giant block and carefully chip pieces off until you're left with single bricks. Hierarchical clustering can be built the exact same two ways.

cards.title: Agglomerative vs. divisive
cards.body: Agglomerative clustering works bottom-up: every data point starts alone, and the algorithm repeatedly merges the closest pair of groups until one cluster remains. Divisive clustering works top-down: it starts with all points in one giant cluster and repeatedly splits off the least similar group until every point is alone.

cards.title: Growing a city vs. slicing a cake
cards.body: Agglomerative clustering is like a city growing from scratch — individual houses (points) slowly combine into neighborhoods, then districts, then one metro area. Divisive clustering is like slicing a whole cake — you start with the entire thing and cut it into smaller and smaller sections.

cards.title: Merging friend groups at a party
cards.body: At a party, agglomerative clustering is like watching pairs of close friends form small circles first, and those circles slowly combine into bigger clusters of people. Divisive clustering, instead, starts by assuming the whole party is one group and slices it into smaller cliques based on who seems least alike.

cards.title: Agglomerative is the default, not divisive
cards.body: You might assume both directions get used equally. In reality, almost every hierarchical clustering tool defaults to agglomerative (bottom-up) because merging two close groups is cheap to compute, while divisive clustering has to consider every possible way to split a big group, which gets expensive fast.

cards.title: Bottom-Up vs. Top-Down
cards.body: The two directions, side by side:

left.text:
Start: every point alone
Repeatedly merge closest pairs
End: one big cluster

right.text:
Start: one big cluster
Repeatedly split least-similar group
End: every point alone

exercises.prompt: Which clustering direction starts with every data point as its own cluster?
exercises.explain: Correct — agglomerative starts bottom-up, with every point alone, then merges upward.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Divisive clustering is used far more often than agglomerative clustering in practice.
exercises.explain: False — agglomerative is the far more common default, since merging nearby pairs is cheaper than testing every possible way to split a big group.

exercises.sentence: Agglomerative clustering works ___-up, while divisive clustering works top-down.
exercises.explain: Agglomerative builds bottom-up: from individual points to one big cluster.

exercises.prompt: A dataset starts as one giant group, and the algorithm keeps splitting off the least-similar subgroup. Which approach is this?
exercises.explain: Right — starting with one cluster and splitting it apart top-down is divisive clustering.

exercises.prompt: You're clustering 1 million customer records and want the fastest hierarchical approach. Why would most tools use agglomerative rather than divisive clustering here?

options.text: Agglomerative only has to compare and merge nearby pairs at each step, while divisive would need to test many possible ways to split each huge group
Divisive is always faster for large datasets
Agglomerative requires picking K in advance, which speeds things up
There's no difference in speed between the two

exercises.explain: Exactly! Merging the closest pair is a much cheaper operation than searching for the best way to split a giant group, which is why agglomerative dominates in practice. Next: a completely different way to cluster, based on density instead of merging or splitting.
